title: Coriolanus
description: Coriolanus — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for Shakespeare's Coriolanus — characters, themes, and the world of the Roman tragedy

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: A Citizen of Antium
memes.description: An unnamed citizen of Antium in Act IV Scene V who gives Coriolanus directions to Aufidius's house, unwittingly facilitating the exiled Roman general's defection to the Volscians.

memes.label: A Patrician
memes.description: An unnamed Roman patrician who appears in Act III, Scene I, where he comments on Coriolanus's political missteps and expresses solidarity with him after the confrontation with the tribunes.

memes.label: Adrian
memes.description: A Volscian spy who encounters a Roman spy (Nicanor) on the highway between Rome and Antium in Act IV Scene III, exchanging intelligence about Coriolanus's banishment.

memes.label: Anger
memes.description: Anger as Coriolanus's defining emotion and tragic catalyst — the choler that the tribunes exploit, the rage that drives his banishment, and the fury that ultimately destroys him.

memes.label: Appeal to the People
memes.description: The Roman political ritual in which Coriolanus must publicly display his battle wounds and wear the "gown of humility" to seek the people's voices for the consulship——and his explosive refusal to perform the ritual.

memes.label: Aufidius's Conspiracy
memes.description: The conspiracy led by Tullus Aufidius with fellow conspirators to assassinate Coriolanus in Antium, triggered by jealousy over Coriolanus's military success among the Volscians and his decision to make peace with Rome.

memes.label: Aufidius Welcomes Coriolanus
memes.description: "The banished Coriolanus arrives at Aufidius's house in Antium, reveals his identity, and is warmly received by his former enemy, who offers him joint command of the Volscian forces against Rome."

memes.label: Battle of Corioli
memes.description: The decisive military engagement that earns [[caius-marcius-coriolanus|Caius Marcius]] his *agnomen* "Coriolanus" — a solo assault on the Volscian city of [[corioli|Corioli]] that transforms an anonymous soldier into Rome's greatest living warrior and sets the stage for his political tragedy.

memes.label: Belly Fable
memes.description: Menenius Agrippa's political allegory of the belly and the body's members — the classical justification for patrician rule delivered to the mutinous plebeians in the opening scene of *Coriolanus*.

memes.label: Body Politic
memes.description: The body politic metaphor — in which the state is imagined as a human body with interdependent parts — is given its most famous Shakespearean articulation in Menenius Agrippa's fable of the belly, delivered to pacify the rioting plebeians in the opening scene of Coriolanus.

memes.label: Caius Marcius Coriolanus
memes.description: The central tragic figure of Shakespeare's Coriolanus — Rome's greatest soldier, whose aristocratic pride and contempt for the [[plebeian-rights|plebeians]] lead to [[coriolanus-banishment|banishment]], [[coriolanus-defection|defection]] to the [[tullus-aufidius|Volscians]], and [[death-of-coriolanus|death]].

relations.label: contempt for the plebeian constitutional order

memes.label: Characters Index
memes.description: Complete index of all characters in Shakespeare's Coriolanus, sorted alphabetically.

memes.label: Class Conflict
memes.description: The social and political struggle between the patrician aristocracy and the plebeian commoners in Republican Rome — the central political dynamic of Coriolanus.

memes.label: Concepts & Ideas Index
memes.description: Index of political concepts, cultural ideas, and dramatic themes explored in Shakespeare's Coriolanus.

memes.label: Conspirators with Aufidius
memes.description: A faction of Volscian conspirators loyal to Tullus Aufidius who orchestrate and execute the assassination of Coriolanus in the play's final scene.

memes.label: Consulship
memes.description: The supreme executive magistracy of Republican Rome — the office Coriolanus seeks, the tribunes obstruct, and whose contested election precipitates the play's central crisis.

memes.label: 科利奥兰纳斯独闯城门
memes.description: Act I Scene IV–V 中，Marcius 在科利奥里攻城战中单枪匹马冲入敌军城门并生还，成为整场战役的转折点，奠定了其军事英雄的传奇形象，并直接促成了"Coriolanus"尊号的授予。

memes.label: Banishment of Coriolanus
memes.description: The pivotal political event of Shakespeare's *Coriolanus* — the [[tribunate|tribunes']] engineered expulsion of Rome's greatest soldier, which transforms the [[caius-marcius-coriolanus|protagonist]] from Roman patriot into [[coriolanus-defection|Volscian ally]] and sets the tragedy on its irreversible course toward [[death-of-coriolanus|death]].

memes.label: Coriolanus in Antium
memes.description: "Coriolanus's arrival in Antium — disguised and in mean apparel — after his banishment, culminating in his dramatic entrance into Tullus Aufidius's house to offer his services to his former enemy."

memes.label: Coriolanus Refuses Spoils
memes.description: "After the victory at Corioli, Cominius offers Coriolanus a tenth of all spoils as reward, but Coriolanus refuses all material recompense, asking only for the freedom of a poor Volscian host who had sheltered him."

memes.label: Corioli
memes.description: The Volscian city whose capture earns [[caius-marcius-coriolanus|Caius Marcius]] his *agnomen* "Coriolanus" — a walled stronghold south of [[rome|Rome]] that becomes the site of both his greatest triumph and [[death-of-coriolanus|his death]].

memes.label: Courage
memes.description: The martial virtue of courage (valour) in Coriolanus — its centrality to Roman identity, its relationship to other virtues, and its limitations as a guide to civic life.

memes.label: Creation of the Tribunate
memes.description: "The establishment of the plebeian tribunate — five tribunes elected to defend the people's interests — in response to the corn riot, marking a fundamental shift in Rome's political structure."

memes.label: Death
memes.description: Death in Coriolanus — the constant presence that defines martial honour, the ultimate sanction of political conflict, and the terminus that awaits the hero who cannot imagine his own mortality.

memes.label: Display of Coriolanus's Wounds
memes.description: "The ceremonial display of Coriolanus's battle wounds before the Roman Senate in Act II Scene II — a traditional requirement for consular candidacy that Coriolanus resists and ultimately evades."

memes.label: "Dramatis Personae"
memes.description: "Character list for Coriolanus"

memes.label: Envy
memes.description: Envy — particularly the martial envy between Coriolanus and Aufidius — functions as the emotional engine of the play's second half, transforming a story of political conflict into a personal tragedy of rivalry and mutual destruction.

memes.label: Events Index
memes.description: Complete index of all key events in Shakespeare's Coriolanus, from battlefield to political arena.

memes.label: Exile
memes.description: The Roman punishment of exilium — banishment from the city — a central dramatic event in Coriolanus that transforms the hero's relationship to Rome.

memes.label: Fame
memes.description: The pursuit of fame and renown as a driving force in Coriolanus's character and the play's exploration of martial honour's relationship to public recognition.

memes.label: Famine
memes.description: The material condition of grain scarcity that drives the opening revolt in Coriolanus — a visceral crisis of subsistence that the political institutions of Rome prove unable to resolve.

memes.label: 科利奥兰纳斯的告别
memes.description: Act IV Scene I 中，Coriolanus 被罗马放逐后与母亲 Volumnia、妻子 Virgilia 及友人在城门口告别，是全剧最具情感张力的家庭场景之一。

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: Fear as political instrument and psychological force in Coriolanus — the tribunes' weapon, the patricians' anxiety, and the emotion the hero refuses to feel.

memes.label: Featured Content
memes.description: The highest-quality pages in the Coriolanus wiki — featured and premium entries selected for depth, citation density, and readability.

memes.label: Fifth Citizen
memes.description: An unnamed Roman citizen who appears in Act II Scene III, granting his voice to Coriolanus for the consulship.

memes.label: First Citizen
memes.description: The lead spokesman of the mutinous Roman citizens who rise up against the patricians at the beginning of the play, demanding corn during the famine and delivering the play's most concentrated critique of patrician rule.

memes.label: First Conspirator
memes.description: One of the three conspirators in Aufidius's faction who plot and execute the assassination of Coriolanus in Act V Scene VI.

memes.label: First Lord
memes.description: A Volscian lord who appears in Act V, Scene VI at Antium, where he participates in the council that witnesses Coriolanus's assassination by Aufidius and the conspirators.

memes.label: First Officer
memes.description: A Roman military officer who appears in Act II, Scene II to prepare the Capitol for Coriolanus's consular confirmation, and who offers a critical assessment of Coriolanus's character in dialogue with his colleague.

memes.label: First Roman
memes.description: An unnamed Roman soldier who appears in Act I Scene V carrying spoils from the captured city of Corioli.

memes.label: First Senator
memes.description: A generic senatorial role in Coriolanus representing both the Roman and Volscian senates; in Rome, a voice of patrician authority; among the Volscians, a strategic military counsellor.

memes.label: First Servingman
memes.description: A servant in Tullus Aufidius's household who interacts with Coriolanus during his visit to Antium and provides comic commentary after Aufidius and Coriolanus's meeting.

memes.label: First Soldier
memes.description: A Roman soldier in Marcius's army who witnesses and reports on Coriolanus's solitary heroism at the gates of Corioli in Act I.

memes.label: Forum Confrontation
memes.description: The public confrontation in the Roman Forum where Coriolanus is challenged by the tribunes Brutus and Sicinius over his contempt for the common citizens, leading to his banishment from Rome.

memes.label: Fourth Citizen
memes.description: A plebeian citizen in Coriolanus, appearing in the Forum scene (Act II, Scene III); the most direct and plain-spoken of the citizens, delivering blunt truths about Coriolanus's character.

memes.label: Gentlewoman
memes.description: The gentlewoman attending on Virgilia, who announces Valeria's visit in Act I, Scene 3, representing the domestic sphere of the play.

memes.label: Hatred
memes.description: Hatred as the binding social force in Coriolanus — between patrician and plebeian, Roman and Volscian, Coriolanus and Aufidius — and the political weapon deployed to destroy the hero.

memes.label: Ingratitude
memes.description: The accusation of ingratitude — directed at Rome by its soldiers and at the plebeians by the patricians — serves as a recurring moral charge throughout the play, framing political conflict in the language of personal betrayal.

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: The contested meaning of justice in Coriolanus — as legal process, as personal retribution, and as the fragile boundary between civic order and the violence it seeks to contain.

memes.label: Lictors
memes.description: The lictors who appear in Act II Scene II as ceremonial attendants to the Roman consuls and senators, representing the formal authority and dignity of the Roman state.

memes.label: Lieutenant to Aufidius
memes.description: A Volscian officer who serves as Tullus Aufidius's confidant, voicing early warnings about Coriolanus's growing influence over the Volscian army (023-002).

memes.label: Loyalty
memes.description: The bond of allegiance tying citizen to state, soldier to commander, and individual to family — tested to destruction by Coriolanus's exile and defection to the Volscians.

memes.label: Mars
memes.description: The Roman god of war serves as Coriolanus's mythological double throughout the play — an archetype of martial prowess that simultaneously elevates the hero to divine status and marks his fatal unsuitability for civil life.

memes.label: Menenius Agrippa
memes.description: Rome's wise patrician elder and surrogate father to [[caius-marcius-coriolanus|Coriolanus]] — a master of rhetoric whose famous "belly fable" calms the [[corn-riot|plebeian revolt]] but whose political wisdom cannot save his protégé from destruction.

memes.label: Mercy
memes.description: The contested role of mercy in Coriolanus — its absence in the protagonist, its power when exercised through maternal intervention, and its complex relationship with Roman honour.

memes.label: Messenger
memes.description: Collective character page for the Messengers who deliver crucial news at key turning points throughout Coriolanus, appearing across all five acts.

memes.label: Military Honor
memes.description: The governing value system of Shakespeare's *Coriolanus* — a code of martial excellence (*[[virtus|virtus]]*) measured in wounds, scars, and battlefield deeds that defines [[caius-marcius-coriolanus|Coriolanus's]] identity but proves fatally incompatible with the demands of [[tribunate|republican politics]].

memes.label: Motherhood
memes.description: The political force of Roman motherhood in *Coriolanus* — Volumnia's maternal authority, the Roman matron ideal, and the paradox of a mother who sent her son to war and demands he destroy the state that shaped him.

memes.label: Name
memes.description: The significance of naming in Coriolanus — how the hero's identity is forged in the giving of his name and destroyed in its loss.

memes.label: 科利奥兰纳斯命名
memes.description: Act I Scene IX–X 中，Cominius 在罗马军营为战功卓著的 Caius Marcius 授予"Coriolanus"尊号，并在罗马广场由传令官公开宣告，标志其身份从罗马将领升格为民族英雄，也为其悲剧性政治命运埋下伏笔。

memes.label: Nature
memes.description: The concept of "nature" as innate, unchangeable character in Coriolanus — the tragic hero's defining trait and the justification for his refusal to compromise.

memes.label: Nicanor
memes.description: A Roman who defects to the Volscians and provides intelligence to Aufidius about Coriolanus's movements in Act IV, Scene 3 — a mirror-figure whose betrayal foreshadows Coriolanus's own defection.

memes.label: Patrician Power
memes.description: The political, social, and ideological dominance of the patrician aristocracy in Republican Rome — the structure against which the tribunate and plebeian rights are asserted in Coriolanus.

memes.label: Patricians
memes.description: The hereditary aristocratic order of [[rome|Republican Rome]] — the ruling class whose [[military-honor|martial values]] and political dominance are challenged by the [[plebeian-rights|plebeian movement]] in Shakespeare's *Coriolanus*.

memes.label: Peace
memes.description: The concept and dramatic role of peace in Coriolanus — a state the warrior hero cannot adapt to, and the ironic absence that defines his tragedy.

memes.label: Places Index
memes.description: Index of all locations in Shakespeare's Coriolanus — Rome, Corioli, Antium and the battlefields between them.

memes.label: Plebeian Attack on Brutus
memes.description: "The plebeians' violent turn against tribune Junius Brutus in Act V Scene IV, dragging him through the streets when hope of salvation arrives with the Roman women's successful embassy to Coriolanus."

memes.label: Plebeian Rebellion
memes.description: The plebeian uprising against the patricians that opens the play, sparked by grain shortages and class resentment, calmed by Menenius's belly fable, and met with Coriolanus's contemptuous scorn.

memes.label: Plebeian Rights
memes.description: The legal and political rights of the Roman plebeian class — the right to elect tribunes, to assemble, and to fair distribution of public resources — fiercely contested throughout Coriolanus.

memes.label: Plebeians
memes.description: The common people of [[rome|Republican Rome]] — the labouring class whose struggle for [[plebeian-rights|political rights]] and economic justice drives the political conflict of Shakespeare's *Coriolanus* and whose collective power, embodied in the [[tribunate|tribunate]], destroys the play's aristocratic protagonist.

memes.label: Populism
memes.description: The political technique of appealing directly to the common people and mobilizing popular sentiment — practiced by both tribunes and patricians in Coriolanus, with sharply divergent motives and consequences.

memes.label: Power
memes.description: Power in Coriolanus — who holds it, how it is exercised, and the irreconcilable conflict between aristocratic and popular conceptions of legitimate rule.

memes.label: Pride
memes.description: The towering self-regard that defines Coriolanus — simultaneously the source of his martial greatness and the fatal flaw that makes him politically impossible.

memes.label: Revenge
memes.description: Revenge transforms the political conflict of the play's first half into the military crisis of its second half, as Coriolanus's personal vendetta against Rome threatens to destroy the city that banished him.

memes.label: Rhetoric
memes.description: The art of political persuasion in Republican Rome — mastered by the tribunes, practiced by Menenius, and conspicuously refused by Coriolanus, whose contempt for rhetoric is itself a rhetorical posture.

memes.label: Roman Constitution
memes.description: The mixed constitution of Republican Rome, balancing monarchical, aristocratic, and democratic elements — the political framework within which Coriolanus's tragedy unfolds.

memes.label: 罗马使团求见科利奥兰纳斯
memes.description: Act V Scene I–II 中，罗马先后派遣 Cominius 和 Menenius 前往 Coriolanus 的营地求情，但均遭拒绝，显示 Coriolanus 对罗马的决绝态度。

memes.label: Roman Herald
memes.description: A minor Roman official who announces Coriolanus's triumph and confers his new name upon his return from Corioli — a ceremonial mouthpiece whose ritual pronouncement transforms Caius Marcius into Coriolanus (011-005).

memes.label: Roman Herald Proclaims Coriolanus
memes.description: "A Roman herald formally proclaims Coriolanus's deeds and new name before the people of Rome during the triumphal entry ceremony, marking the public ratification of his military honor."

memes.label: Roman Honour
memes.description: The Roman concept of *honour* as a martial and civic virtue — the value system that makes and unmakes Coriolanus, transforming him from Rome's greatest hero into its deadliest enemy.

memes.label: Roman Patricians
memes.description: The patrician class of Rome — the hereditary nobility who govern the Roman Republic and whose conflict with the plebeians drives the political dimension of the tragedy.

memes.label: Roman Senate
memes.description: The supreme political council of Republican Rome — the assembly of patrician elders whose authority over war, peace, and the consulship provides the institutional framework for the political conflict of Shakespeare's *Coriolanus*.

memes.label: Rome
memes.description: The political and physical stage of Shakespeare's *Coriolanus* — a republican city-state where [[class-conflict|patrician and plebeian forces]] contend for power, and the arena that transforms military glory into political tragedy.

memes.label: Sacrifice
memes.description: The surrender of self-interest for a greater cause — military (Coriolanus at Corioli), political (his banishment), and familial (Volumnia's final intervention) — a recurring and contested motif in Coriolanus.

memes.label: Second Citizen
memes.description: The more moderate of the two named citizens who open the play, serving as a cautious counterweight to the First Citizen's radical demands and defending Coriolanus's military service.

memes.label: Second Conspirator
memes.description: One of the three conspirators in Aufidius's faction who propose and carry out the assassination of Coriolanus in Act V Scene VI.

memes.label: Second Lord
memes.description: A Volscian lord present in Act V, Scene VI at Antium, who attempts to restrain the violence against Coriolanus and later tempers the judgment of Aufidius after the assassination.

memes.label: Second Messenger
memes.description: A Roman messenger who delivers the news of the Volscian invasion and later announces the women's successful embassy to Coriolanus in Act V.

memes.label: Second Officer
memes.description: A Roman military officer who appears alongside the First Officer in Act II, Scene II, and who delivers an eloquent defense of Coriolanus's achievements against the First Officer's critique.

memes.label: Second Patrician
memes.description: An unnamed Roman patrician in Act III Scene I who, alongside A Patrician and Menenius, witnesses Coriolanus's confrontation with the tribunes and expresses weary exasperation at the political turmoil.

memes.label: Second Roman
memes.description: An unnamed Roman soldier who takes spoils from the captured city of Corioli in Act I Scene V.

memes.label: Second Senator
memes.description: A generic senatorial role in Coriolanus appearing as a supporting voice in both Roman and Volscian senates; notable for guarding the Volscian gates against Menenius in Act V.

memes.label: Second Servingman
memes.description: A servant in Tullus Aufidius's household who, with the First and Third Servingmen, attempts to expel the disguised Coriolanus from Aufidius's house and later discusses his qualities.

memes.label: Second Soldier
memes.description: An unnamed Roman soldier at the siege of Corioli who, along with First Soldier, refuses to follow Marcius into the enemy city, testifying to the common soldier's instinct for self-preservation.

memes.label: Senate Debates Coriolanus's Consulship
memes.description: "The Roman Senate debates and confirms Coriolanus as consul, with Cominius delivering a eulogy of his battlefield deeds, followed by the contentious requirement that he seek the people's voices."

memes.label: Servingmen
memes.description: The three servants of Tullus Aufidius who provide comic relief and choric commentary on Coriolanus's defection to the Volscians in Act IV Scene V.

memes.label: Seventh Citizen
memes.description: An unnamed Roman citizen who, in Act II Scene III, gives the final individual voice for Coriolanus's consulship in the Forum.

memes.label: 马歇斯与奥菲迪乌斯单挑
memes.description: Act I Scene VIII 中，Caius Marcius 与伏尔斯将领 Tullus Aufidius 在战场上的首次正面对决，两人互表憎恨后交锋，奠定全剧最核心的宿敌关系。

memes.label: Sixth Citizen
memes.description: An unnamed Roman citizen who supports Coriolanus's consulship bid in Act II Scene III, affirming his noble deeds.

memes.label: Third Citizen
memes.description: A plebeian citizen in Coriolanus who appears in the Forum scene (Act II, Scene III); the most eloquent and self-aware of the citizens, known for witty, paradoxical reflections on the nature of the multitude.

memes.label: Third Conspirator
memes.description: The most strategically articulate of the three conspirators in Aufidius's faction; provides the tactical rationale for assassinating Coriolanus before he can address the people.

memes.label: Third Roman
memes.description: An unnamed Roman soldier in Act I Scene V who laments mistaking base metal for silver among the spoils of Corioli, prompting Marcius's contemptuous rebuke of the looting soldiers.

memes.label: Third Servingman
memes.description: A servant in Tullus Aufidius's household who confronts the disguised Coriolanus, is beaten away, and later brings news from inside Aufidius's house about Coriolanus's reception.

memes.label: Treachery
memes.description: The accusation of treachery as a political weapon in Coriolanus — deployed against the hero by the tribunes and against him again by Aufidius, framing his tragic end.

memes.label: Treaty of Peace
aliases: Peace of Rome and the Volscians

memes.label: Tribunate
memes.description: The plebeian constitutional office at the heart of Shakespeare's *Coriolanus* — created after the [[first-secession|First Secession]], the tribunate embodies the [[plebeian-rights|people's power]] against [[patrician-power|patrician dominance]], and its holders [[sicinius-velutus|Sicinius]] and [[junius-brutus|Brutus]] become the political antagonists who destroy [[caius-marcius-coriolanus|Coriolanus]].

memes.label: Tribunes Manipulate the Plebeians
memes.description: "Sicinius and Brutus systematically manipulate the plebeian citizens to turn against Coriolanus, engineering his political downfall through strategic disinformation and procedural exploitation."

memes.label: Tribunes' Plot
memes.description: The political conspiracy orchestrated by tribunes Brutus and Sicinius to manipulate public opinion against Coriolanus and engineer his banishment from Rome, exploiting his inability to perform political humility.

memes.label: Virtus
memes.description: The Roman concept of manly excellence — the fusion of courage, martial skill, moral worth, and devotion to the res publica — embodied by Coriolanus in its purest and most destructive form.

memes.label: Voices
memes.description: The "voices" of the Roman people — the electoral currency of republican politics, transformed by Shakespeare into a bitter pun that exposes the emptiness of democratic ritual when the candidate despises the electorate.

memes.label: Volsce
memes.description: A Volscian agent in Coriolanus who meets the Roman spy Nicanor on the road between Rome and Antium; his encounter yields critical intelligence about Rome's internal divisions that precipitates the Volscian invasion.

memes.label: Volscian Declaration of War
memes.description: "The Volscian declaration of war against Rome, announced in Act I Scene I, setting in motion the military campaign that leads to the battle of Corioli and the elevation of Caius Marcius."

memes.label: Volscians
memes.description: The rival Italic people whose military conflict with [[rome|Rome]] frames the martial action of Shakespeare's *Coriolanus* — the enemy state that [[caius-marcius-coriolanus|Coriolanus]] first conquers at [[corioli|Corioli]] and later serves after his [[coriolanus-banishment|banishment]], ultimately becoming the instrument of [[death-of-coriolanus|his death]].

memes.label: War
memes.description: War as the defining backdrop of Coriolanus — the arena where Roman martial virtue is forged, tested, and ultimately made politically obsolete.

memes.label: Wounds
memes.description: The wounds of war as dramatic symbol in *Coriolanus* — scars that testify to martial virtue but become, in the electoral ritual, a site of contested meaning between patrician honour and plebeian demand.

memes.label: Young Marcius
memes.description: The young son of Caius Marcius Coriolanus and Virgilia — a minor but symbolically potent figure whose silent presence at the climax of the play breaks Coriolanus's resolve and seals his fate.
